intro:
The mount of the congregation appears in Isaiah 14:13 within the taunt against the king of Babylon — I will sit on the mount of the congregation, in the far reaches of the north — as part of the five I will assertions of the one who sought to ascend to the heights of God. The phrase likely reflects ancient Near Eastern mythological geography, where the divine assembly was believed to convene on a cosmic mountain in the north, corresponding to the Canaanite conception of Mount Zaphon as the dwelling of the gods.

Isaiah uses this background polemically: the pride of Babylon aspired to usurp the place of divine authority, but the taunt ends with the tyrant brought down to Sheol. The phrase may also carry resonances with the Israelite understanding of Zion as the mountain where God dwells and meets with his people — reflected in the tent of meeting (Exodus 25:22; Exodus 29:42–43) where God promised to meet Israel and speak to Moses. In this reading the mount of the congregation is the proper realm of God alone, which no human or earthly power can usurp without facing divine judgment.
